display.purpose: Hero name and flagship technical proof only.

shadows.name: soft
shadows.purpose: Quiet proof cards and low elevation.

shadows.name: medium
shadows.purpose: Featured blocks, modals, and inspected cards.

motion.name: ease-out-engineering
motion.purpose: Responsive UI entrance, hover, and active feedback.

motion.name: ease-in-out-engineering
motion.purpose: Rare coordinated movement where orientation matters.

components.name: Primary Button
components.description: Primary action for resume download and contact.

components.name: Metric Chip
components.description: Compact proof label for telemetry, architecture, and role-fit metrics.

components.name: Proof Card
components.description: Evidence container for project, value, and experience proof.

narrative.overview: The portfolio should feel like a calm engineering console assembled for a serious technical review: clear hierarchy, high-confidence typography, exact metrics, and visible system architecture.
keyCharacteristics: Crisp blue/teal engineering palette with large neutral surfaces.
Sora display type for technical authority and Manrope for readable body copy.
Cards only where they frame repeated evidence or case-study detail.
Motion is restrained, fast, and explanatory; it never blocks scanning.

rules.name: The Signal Color Rule
rules.body: Blue and teal are reserved for actions, proof, and system relationships.

rules.name: The Briefing Type Rule
rules.body: Large type introduces the page; compact type carries evidence.

rules.name: The Inspection Rule
rules.body: Elevation appears when a user is inspecting or selecting something.

dos: Do keep HPI visa and no-sponsorship language visible in the hero, footer, and contact section.
Do make TelloTwin the flagship proof object with 10 Hz telemetry, a 17-field state model, 12 REST endpoints, and one WebSocket stream.
Do frame research and publications as engineering evidence, not as an academic destination.
donts: Don't use university-application positioning, degree-application language, or professor-facing calls to action.
Don't claim unverified telemetry rates or other unverified performance.
Don't let cards become the whole design; use technical diagrams, timelines, and metrics to create hierarchy.
